speaker: interviewer
text: Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my daughter Rodi. And this is her husband Marvin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Rodi. I understand that you were born in Holland. And we would like to hear your memories of being a child during the occupation. And just share with us what your experiences were.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. One of the first memories I have was that Amsterdam, or at least on our street, we were being occupied by the Germans. And we were in the family store in the family business. And we had to stay behind shades, or whatever they were, because we were not allowed to be seen as the Germans were marching through our street. And I remember that time. I might not have remembered-- realized that that was the occupation. But it was the occupation. And I remember being taken from nursery school by a friend of the family because the Germans were going to occupy or kill all the children in the nursery school. And my mother got wind of this and sent this friend with her bicycle. And she-- and it never materialized. But it could have happened. And I also remember that-- we always were a very close family. So we-- you know, my mother's sisters and brothers. They were not married until 1940. So we were all very, very close. And my aunt-- my youngest aunt's husband was taken away from her and her mother-in-law stayed with her. He was sent to Mauthasen ultimately and killed there. And her mother-in-law stayed with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't remember how I broke my arm, but I did break my arm. And I-- since my mother and father were always working, my aunt took me to her house. And I spent weeks there and enjoyed it very much. And her mother-in-law taught me to read. Which was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shall we say [NON-ENGLISH], as we say in Jewish? Because I was-- never did go back to school, and I would not have learned how to read for a long, long time. So I guess it was meant to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to-- and I was four years old at the time. And then I remember my birthday party at my mom's house, also during the war. And my uncles-- it was my fifth birthday and was before we went-- we were taken to camp. And the whole family was still there. And that was the last time we were all together. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember wearing the Jewish star. I-- I remember the first time we were taken to camp, because I can still remember my mother's handbag. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was crying. And you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken-- I still remember, I was sleeping, and they woke us up and knocked on the door and took the three of us away. And we went to Westerbork. And I also, even though I was as young as I was, I was still-- I remember having to walk from Hochhalen to Westerbork-- with the Germans, of course, the soldiers accompanying us. My mom and I stayed in the cabin together with somebody else who I don't remember who it was. My father was in a different barrack. And we really had nothing with us. We had-- we were taken from our house the way we were. And I also remember when we were in the truck, and we stopped somewhere else. It was an army truck. And there were other people in there. And a woman, a non-Jewish woman who saw it happening came and gave me some fruit. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I don't remember what we did in the camp every day. We were only there a short while. And through different reasons, which I'm sure my mother has explained, we were sent back home. And our lives continued. For me, as a child, it was somewhat normal. You know, because if you have your parents and your grandparents and your aunts and uncles and everybody around to love you, you don't realize that these bad things are happening. And then I remember being taken the second time with my grandparents and my parents. And we were again in Westerbork for a short time. Excuse me. And then, we came back again. And then, one night, the Germans had taken our-- the family business away. And the German who was occupying our store, he was a Dutch Nazi. He was not the-- the German was sent to the front to fight and the Dutch Nazi that he took to replace us was then decided, well, it was time to get rid of us. And they took us to what you used to be a theater. And we all went there. And the whole family was there. My aunts, my uncles. Everybody who was still around Amsterdam was there. And they had taken all the seats out. And I remember my one uncle schlepping mattresses. I remember that. And my grandmother, she was a-- she was a cutie. And she says, oh my God, my food is burning. It was Friday night, and her Shabbat dinner was going down the tubes big time because it was nobody there to take care of it. I don't know what eventually happened to it. And that was her main concern. You know, her family shouldn't eat. And then we were let go. And I remember the German counting us out of the theater, and saying schweindhund, which means pig. [GERMAN] You know, I still remember that. And-- and then after that, we were taken away from our homes, and sent to the east side of Amsterdam so that they'd have all the few Jews that were still available, to have them together so they wouldn't have to go chasing all over for us. And that was the last time that we were in Amsterdam for the wartime. And I remember we were on the train station. And we had a family whistle. And everybody was whistling, because they all knew if they got a whistle back, that there was somebody else there because there was hundreds and hundreds of people there. And then we realized that everybody was there. We also-- and the family still does this. It's cute in the way, I guess. I don't know. Maybe it's not important. But they also, you know, rang the bell three times. Because if you heard a bell three times, that would mean it was family. If it rang once, that means you better watch out before you open the door. That was when we were still in Amsterdam. It was just an identification. And to this day, they still ring each other's bells three times. [LAUGHS] And then I remember, you know, being in camp. We in the same barrack with my Aunt Margie, my youngest aunt whose husband was killed in Mauthausen. And the three of us stayed together, and my father-- the men were in different barracks, and my other family were in different barracks. And they had to work in the kitchen, and they had to do things. And I used to go into the kitchen and sometimes steal carrots or potatoes and, you know hide under their legs, so we'd have something to eat. I truthfully must say I never starved to death. But I don't remember what I ate, but whatever it was, it kept us alive. I do remember the Germans coming into the barrack and calling out people's names in the middle of the night that went on transport to Auschwitz. [CRYING] From then on, we were, as my mother must've explained, how we went to the internment camp in Vittel in France. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- there were English people there. There were Dutch people. There were people from all over. And that's when I finally got a little education. Because there was a convent of English nuns that were also prisoners. But they ran a school. And we got a little education. And we also had to clean the convent. I still remember scrubbing their bathrooms. And I don't know, I was maybe seven years old on my hands and knees. It was good training, I suppose. And-- but they were-- that was good. You know, it was-- it was a ray of hope, and a ray of sunshine. And in the camp itself, in Westerbork, I don't remember seeing anybody beaten. You know, we ourselves were never beaten up. I mean, I guess it was the best of-- of what you could have under the circumstances. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, I-- I realized that we weren't free. But I really didn't think it was-- you know, I really didn't, at that moment, suffer, because again, because we had our family. And everybody that was familiar. I was, thank God, with my parents and my grandparents, and my aunts and uncles. We were all in the same place. Which was a salvation. And then-- so in-- in Vittel, we went-- then we were liberated in 1944 by the French underground. And they couldn't hold on to-- you know, the Germans were still fighting with their last breath. And they defeated the maquis, which is the French underground. And the Germans came back. And they were-- we had a to watch our step because they were really mad, you know, and they knew they were losing. And were-- And then the Americans came and liberated us. They took over our camp. And we-- the rest of Europe was not free. At least Holland was not. So we had to move further south to a place called La Bourboule. And they had hotels. Because it was really-- it's a-- it's a resort with thermal-- you know, it's a ski area. And they had hotels. And it was the only place they could put that many people. And in order to survive, we got packages from the Red Cross. And I went to the French school. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the principal of the school befriended my mom. She loved my mom. Madame [NON-ENGLISH]. And she invited us over for tea. I mean, tea-- it was like, my God. The queen is asking us to visit, you know? And she was just-- I mean, she gave me a book which I still have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- my husband and I went back one day, because I wanted to see the town. And she had just died. She was-- you know, nobody even-- you know, it was an old man who remembered her. And I went to the school and I went in the classroom. And I learned some French. Because they didn't speak English, of course. And I learned to speak English from the English nuns in Vittel. You know, I had-- so I knew some English when I went back to Holland. And so I got some education in La Bourboule. But I was still behind for children my age. And then when we knew that Holland was liberated, my mom went to Paris, near Paris and got us papers. And we went back to Holland. And as my mom must've said, we were taken in by these people. And I had to go to school to catch-- to catch up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was-- the school I went to, a school to catch up. Then I went to a regular grammar school. Where I-- my mom had told the teacher to give me extra lessons so I could-- you know I had to do like do three years in one, or something like that. I mean it was, you know, had to learn fast. And the-- there were some children in the school who were, through their parents' fault, were not very happy about seeing Jews. You know, and every so often, remarks were made. Which was most painful. But you learn to live with that too I guess. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know-- and I think because of the-- of the war, I never made any close relationships. Because I wasn't raised with any of these children. And I never made any close friendships. Because there was nobody that wanted to be friendly. Or maybe I didn't want to be friendly. I don't know. But it was just-- there were no-- you know, I didn't go to kindergarten or anywhere with these people. I was like a stranger. And then I went-- I had to take an entrance examination to get to the high school in Amsterdam. And I was-- I passed that. And then I went to school for three years, to the gymnasium. And we had to study very hard. It was-- it's very-- it's much harder than here. And you learn more, but you also have to study your life away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in-- when I was nine years old-- well, this was going back a little-- my uncle, one of my mother's brothers, lived in England. And he asked me-- her to send me over. And I flew to England by myself. And my grandma said, now don't get off in the middle. OK? Because nobody had ever been on an airplane in my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was nine-- yeah, nine-- and I had my 10th birthday in England. And he bought me clothes and everything because there was nothing to be gotten in Holland. But anyway, when later on, my mother sent me to England again a couple years later, and it was only because she was afraid that we were going to have another world war with the Russians. And then they decided to leave for the United States. And in 1951, the day before my 15th birthday, I came to the United States. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I guess it was the best thing that could happen to us. It was a very, very good decision on my parents' part. It was a tough decision, because they had to give up-- they had already worked up their business again, which we had lost during the war. And then to start all over again? It's like a crazy life. It's like a roller coaster.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but I went to high school here, and I met my husband right after we graduated. Cause-- and we got married in '55.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in high school, did you feel different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Than the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other children that had gone through the same experiences you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were a few girls who were from German parents. But the parents had come here much earlier. They had come before the war. And everybody was very nice. I was like a curiosity. I was like somebody to look at and to talk about. Not badly, but, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was hard. It was difficult. Because, first of all, I spoke some English. I had learned English in high school in Holland. But that was English. This is American. It was hard to understand. It was hard for me to read the English books, you know, the classics that we had to read. But I guess I caught on and I graduated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was accepted at the University of Illinois at the age of 16. And then when they found out I was 16, I wasn't allowed in. This wouldn't happen in this day and age. But I was too young. So I had to go back to high school. Because there was-- besides the English courses, there wasn't much that I hadn't learned already. But it was OK. I mean, it was a place to go. And there was nothing else I could do. You know, you have to make do with what you make do. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that you talked about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't talk about my experiences to my family or to my friends. And I have a very dear friend who always says, you never talk about it. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I really think that psychologically we try to put it behind us. We're not the type of people-- at least my family is, where I come from-- we don't like to live in the past. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to go on. We had to do what we had to do. I had to go to new schools and I had to go to  a new country, and learn new language and learn new ways. And just fit in and do what you have to do to get on with life. You can't live in the past. I mean at least we've, you know-- that doesn't mean that we shouldn't remember. But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, we were so busy. We were so busy making a new life. And we were busy adjusting to new circumstances. And then I got married. And then we had to make a living. And we-- and we had three children. And we had to work. We had to go on. We had to raise children. We had to do, we had to do. Now, when-- I'm at a stage where, not that I'm not busy, but my mind-- you know, I'm more-- I'm busy with what I do. But I have more time to think. And now it really bothers me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It bothers me that one man could take five years of my life-- five years that I never get back. And the reaction to that? It's not right! It shouldn't be. And it's still happening all over the world to other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this happened to us. That somebody could take-- I see my grandchildren-- thank God. They're beautiful, and we love them to pieces. I never had that life. Not that I'm jealous, or-- but those years of being-- not having to worry about anything, and being free and being always-- that, I did not have. I mean, my parents did a fabulous job. And they always cared for me and protected me. My whole family did. I mean, nothing bad ever befell me. But now, when I think back, this-- this idiot took-- this maniac took five years of our lives. Thank God we survived to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lot of people didn't. But this is-- life shouldn't be like that.  I mean, it shouldn't be. It shouldn't be allowed. And to think that people, governments knew about it, and let it happen is really betrayal. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm so happy and thankful that-- that most of our family did survive on my mother's side. And that I have my mom. And I have a husband and children and grandchildren. It's so much to be grateful for. But at times, it really bothers you. Yeah, we've been married 40 years now. But it's just something that's starts to grate on me. And this particular year was very difficult, because they keep showing on TV-- 50 years. And all the camps and everything. And it's just-- it's just too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: It brings back-- it brings back all the memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find, when you were raising your children, that you talked to them about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My children say I did. But I don't remember. You know? I might have talked about-- I don't know. I don't remember talking about it. You know, I never said eat your food because I starved. I mean, I would never say anything like that. So I don't remember. But they say they know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is most important to you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: To make sure-- to try, if there is any way, that this would never happen again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That would be-- that it would never befall any of our children or grandchildren. Any of us. I don't-- not just mine, just anywhere. I mean, I see the slaughter going on in Yugoslavia and Bosnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- I mean, to see those babies die. That's-- that shouldn't be. You know? It just should not be. I'd like to end by asking you to tell us something special about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the best mom. And I also had the best dad. My dad, [CRYING] may he rest in peace, was my very best friend that I will ever have. I could do no wrong. He didn't spoil me, because he used to beat me up if I was bad. He used to hold up his hand, he says, remember this? But he was a good father. And he was a good friend. And he was fun, and he was just wonderful. And my mom-- and my mom is the best. And-- and my whole family-- my aunts, my uncles, my grandparents. Without them, none of us, you know, all of us hanging together is what made us really be able to survive. And sustain somewhat normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Rodi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Give me a Kleenex.
